coredata.dc:title: Preparation of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their detection of tetracycline antibiotics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Sun, Qian
Yang, Min
Liu, Enzhou
Xue, Weiming
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The massive use of tetracyclines poses a risk to human health and their quantitative testing is urgent. This work provides a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) fluorescent probe which was prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and ethylenediamine as precursors for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was greatly enhanced by the doping of nitrogen. The fabricated N-CQDs exhibited a spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.66 nm. It displayed blue fluorescence, a quantum yield of 10.97 % and an excitation-dependent emission behavior. It also had excellent stability, such as resistance to the effects of varying ionic strength, pH and continuous UV light irradiation. The N-CQDs detect tetracycline antibiotics including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC) based on fluorescence quenching caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) with excellent specificity. The limits of detection (LOD) were 0.344, 0.265, and 0.117 μM, respectively. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of tetracycline antibiotics in rat serum and milk samples with satisfactory recoveries, confirming that the proposed fluorescent probe has practical application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Tetracycline antibiotics
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent response of TC, OTC and CTC
The mechanism for the N-CQDs response to TC, OTC and CTC
Detection of TC, OTC and CTC in real samples

The authors would like to thank not only the Key Science and Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2022TD-3) for their help in the experiments, but also the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276 ). The authors would like to thank not only the Key Science and Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province ( 2022TD-3 ) for their help in the experiments, but also the Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the TEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of (a) different nitrogen sources, (b) content ratios of Glu to EDA, (c) synthesis temperature and (d) reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. All data were obtained at the respective optimal excitation wavelengths.
TEM image (a), HRTEM image (b), XRD curve (c) and FT-IR spectrum (d) of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs. High resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).

(a) 3D fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs. (b) The emission spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. The inset represents the relationship between emission wavelength and excitation wavelength. (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs (the inset is a photo of N-CQDs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)). The photobleaching resistance (d), salt tolerance (e), and pH stability f) of N-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/TC. (b) The linear relationship between fluorescence value and TC concentration in the range of 0–30 μM and 30–100 μM. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/OTC. (d) The linear relationship between fluorescence value and OTC concentration in the range of 0–20 μM and 20–100 μM.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/CTC with CTC concentration in the range of 0–40 μM (a) and 40–400 μM (b). (c) The linear relationship between fluorescence value and CTC concentration in the range of 0–40 μM and 40–200 μM. (d) Fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in the presence of interference and TC coexisted with interference.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs, UV–vis absorption spectra of TC/OTC/CTC. 3D Fluorescence Lifetime of N-CQDs (b), N-CQDs+TC (c), N-CQDs+OTC (d), N-CQDs+CTC with 40 μM (e) and 200 μM (f).
Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of TC.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of N-CQDs and their detection of TC.
Comparison of proposed fluorescent probe with other methods for the detection of TC, OTC and CTC.
Determination results of TC/OTC/CTC in real samples and recovery results.

head.ce:title: Preparation of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their detection of tetracycline antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The massive use of tetracyclines poses a risk to human health and their quantitative testing is urgent. This work provides a novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) fluorescent probe which was prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and ethylenediamine as precursors for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was greatly enhanced by the doping of nitrogen. The fabricated N-CQDs exhibited a spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.66 nm. It displayed blue fluorescence, a quantum yield of 10.97 % and an excitation-dependent emission behavior. It also had excellent stability, such as resistance to the effects of varying ionic strength, pH and continuous UV light irradiation. The N-CQDs detect tetracycline antibiotics including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC) based on fluorescence quenching caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) with excellent specificity. The limits of detection (LOD) were 0.344, 0.265, and 0.117 μM, respectively. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of tetracycline antibiotics in rat serum and milk samples with satisfactory recoveries, confirming that the proposed fluorescent probe has practical application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics, mainly including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC), as a kind of broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agent, have been widely used to promote animal growth and prevent animal diseases in animal husbandry, and to treat infectious diseases in humans [1,2]. However, it is easy to cause residues in the environment and foods of animal origin such as milk, vegetables, meat, and eggs due to the low bioavailability of tetracycline antibiotics in the body and the difficulty of degradation in the environment [3]. The residual antibiotics in the environment and food are eventually enriched into the human body through the food chain and have effects on the health of the organism, such as developing bacterial resistance, liver damage, and affecting dental development [4–6]. Owing to these side effects, multiple analytical methods have been established to detect tetracycline antibiotics, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], chromatograph-mass spectrometer (LC–MS) [8], and the like. However, most of these assays require specialized skills, sophisticated equipment, and time-consuming sample pre-treatment procedures that are not readily available to the general public. Therefore, it is of great importance to human health to construct a rapid, sensitive and simple detection method for tetracycline antibiotics.

In recent years, fluorescence analysis technology has attracted increasing attention because of its advantages such as high sensitivity, short response time, simple operation and low cost. It is widely used for the detection of heavy metals [9], bioactive molecules [10,11], drug molecules [12] and antibiotics [13]. The fluorescent probe carrier includes organic fluorescent dyes, metal-semiconductor quantum dots and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The main drawbacks of organic fluorescent dyes are poor optical stability and water solubility, and small Stokes shift [14]. For metal semiconductors, the main disadvantage is the presence of heavy metal ions, which are potentially biotoxic and environmental pollution hazards [15]. These drawbacks limited their further development in the field of fluorescent probes. Compared with the former two, CQDs not only have superior optical stability and water solubility, but also have unparalleled biosafety, making them more advantageous for applications in the field of fluorescent probes. Therefore, it is more practical to use CQDs as fluorescent probes for detecting tetracycline antibiotics. There are also some reports on the detection of tetracycline antibiotics by CQDs. For example, Fu et al. prepared novel N, P, and B co-doped reduced CQDs by simple hydrothermal treatment and chemical redox reactions for TC detection with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.73 nM [16]; Liu et al. prepared biomass CQDs by sweet potato peels for sensitive detection of OTC in honey with the recoveries ranging from 90.2 % to 97.3 % [17]; Zhang et al. prepared CQDs fluorescent probe by hawthorn for the rapid detection of CTC in pork samples [18]. While the work reported above is certainly a recognizable achievement, these probes can only detect specific tetracycline antibiotics that do not facilitate the detection of widespread used and diverse tetracycline antibiotics. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare fluorescent probes capable of detecting a variety of tetracycline antibiotics such as TC, OTC, and CTC. One of the challenges of CQDs in the field of fluorescent probes is the low sensitivity due to low fluorescence intensity. One of the solutions to this problem is to improve the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield using heteroatom doping such as S, N, P and B to enhance the detection sensitivity [19–21]. Among them, the nitrogen source is the most commonly used element for the preparation of heteroatom doping because the raw materials are abundant, cheap and easily available, and the atomic radius of nitrogen atoms is similar to that of carbon atoms. And to the best of our knowledge, N-CQDs for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics have been rarely reported, although there are more reports on N-CQDs. Therefore, it is necessary to further investigate the nitrogen doping to improve the fluorescence performance of CQDs.

In order to solve the above challenges, herein, N-CQDs fluorescent probes with bright blue fluorescence were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of glucose (Glu) and ethylenediamine (EDA) for the detection of TC, OTC and CTC ( Scheme 1). The doping of nitrogen enhances the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-CQDs were 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence quantum yield was about 10.97 %. The fluorescence of the N-CQDs could be efficiently quenched by TC/OTC/CTC via the IFE, thus a method for detection of TC/OTC/CTC in the range of 0–100/0–100/0–200 μmol/L was established and used to TC determination in milk samples and rat serum with satisfying results.

Glucose (Glu) was acquired from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Sulfuric acid and anhydrous magnesium sulfate were got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.,Ltd. Phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.2–7.4) was purchased from Quanzhou Hongke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instruments information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

As shown in Scheme 1, N-CQDs were prepared via hydrothermal method using Glu and EDA as C and N sources respectively. Briefly, 0.1 g of Glu and 0.15 mL of EDA were dissolved in 50 mL H2O. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon stainless steel autoclave and then reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles of impurities, and the obtained supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h to remove small molecular impurities. The solution in the dialysis bag was dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain a brown solid powder.
To consider the effect of N doping, CQDs were prepared in accordance with the described above method without adding EDA.

The tetracycline antibiotics used in this work included TC, OTC and CTC. Taking TC as an example, the typical detection procedure was as follows: different concentrations of TC stock solutions (0.5 mL) were added to 4.5 mL as-prepared N-CQDs (10 μg/mL), and the final concentration of TC was 0–100 μM, followed by incubation at room temperature for 5 min, and then the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were measured and recorded. The final concentration of TC, OTC and CTC were 0–100 μM, 0–100 μM, and 0–400 μM, respectively. To ensure the credibility of the data, all tests were performed in three parallel groups.
The real sample assay of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

N-CQDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method in this work. To obtain CQDs with excellent optical properties, different nitrogen sources and reaction conditions were investigated. Glucose (Glu) was used as the carbon source, and urea, ethylenediamine (EDA), glycine (Gly) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) were used as the nitrogen sources, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum was shown in Fig. 1a. It was obvious that all the nitrogen sources play a role in enhancing the fluorescence of CQDs, but only EDA has the most significant enhancement effect. The reason that the effect of EDA is the strongest may be related to its molecular structure. The doping of nitrogen sources with different molecular structures may affect the microstructure, electron distribution and surface defects of carbon quantum dots. Among these nitrogen sources, the structures of urea and Gly contain not only nitrogen but also oxygen, and the introduction of oxygen may generate more surface defects associated with oxygen, and their existence can affect the radiative leap of electrons and thus the fluorescence intensity [22,23]. PEI although has more nitrogen, its huge molecular structure may create more space site-resistant, which makes it difficult to dope the nitrogen atoms into the backbone of CQDs, and therefore has limited improvement on fluorescence intensity. In contrast, EDA has a much smaller structure than PEI and does not contain oxygen, which is probably the reason for its optimal performance. The above explanation is a preliminary speculation, and more precise reasons need to be explored in future studies. Furthermore, the effect of the ratio of Glu and EDA on the fluorescence of N-CQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1b, when the mass of Glu was 0.1 g, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was gradually enhanced as the amount of EDA increased. When the amount of EDA was 0.15 mL, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was the strongest, and then decreased gradually as the amount of EDA increased. Therefore, 0.15 mL EDA was selected for subsequent experiments. In addition, the influence of hydrothermal synthesis temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs were also studied. The experimental results (Fig. 1c and d) showed that the fluorescence of the prepared N-CQDs gradually increased with the increase of synthesis temperature and reaction time. Considering energy and time consumption, the optimum synthetic temperature finally selected to be 200 °C, and reaction time selected to be 4 h. Under the optimum synthetic conditions, N-CQDs were synthesized and characterized.

The morphology of the N-CQDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2a, N-CQDs were evenly dispersed without obvious agglomeration, with an approximate spherical appearance and an average particle size of 2.66 nm. In addition, HRTEM image of N-CQDs (Fig. 2b) showed a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm and a good crystallinity corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite carbon [12]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) displayed a broad diffraction peak at 21.3◦, which was consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of the carbon-based materials [24]. The above results revealed that the synthesized N-CQDs had the highly crystalline and graphite structure. The surface groups were examined using FT-IR (Fig. 2d) and revealed the following peaks: N-H (3428 cm−1), O-H (3072 cm−1), C-H (2941 cm−1), -COOH (1639–1566 cm−1), and C-OH (1336 cm−1) [25,26]. The elemental composition of N-CQDs was studied by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited three characteristic peaks at 285.08, 399.08, and 532.08 eV for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The percentages of three elements were calculated to be 70.27 % C, 15.83 % N and 13.90 % O in N-CQDs, indicating that N-CQDs were oxygen-rich and nitrogen-rich. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum ( Fig. 3b) confirmed the presences of CC/C-C (284.83 eV), C-N/C-O (286.13 eV) and CN/CO (287.73 eV) [27]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) reflected the presences of C-NC (399.48 eV) and -NH2 (400.63 eV) [28]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) evidenced the presences of C–O (531.33 eV) and CO (532.13 eV). FT-IR and XPS results revealed that the prepared N-CQDs were rich in hydrophilic functional groups and had good water solubility, which was conducive to their further application studies.

In order to investigate the optical property of N-CQDs, the 3D fluorescence, 2D fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy were performed. As shown in Fig. 4a, the 3D fluorescence spectrum showed that the maximum fluorescence center was located at (360, 445). Asymmetric fluorescence emission circles predicted that N-CQDs were excitation-dependent. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the excitation wavelength of N-CQDs increased from 300 nm to 420 nm, the emission wavelength of N-CQDs increased from 418 nm to 485 nm. The emission wavelength is linearly dependent on the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity was maximum at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the emission wavelength of 445 nm. The excitation-dependent behavior of N-CQDs was caused by the different sizes and surface defects of the nanoparticles, which was the same as that of most fluorescent CQDs [29]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the prepared N-CQDs displayed blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were located at 360 nm and 445 nm, respectively. The adsorption peak below 300 nm was attributed to the π-π * transition of the N-CQD carbon core, which implied the formation of the conjugated framework [30]. The absorption at 300–450 nm was assigned to N and O doped surface states or energy traps induced by n-π * transitions of CO/CN [31]. In addition, the relative photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared N-CQDs at 360 nm wavelength was detected as 10.97 % using quinine sulfate as a reference object (Details are in Fig. S1 and Table S1). The stability of N-CQDs was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs under high ion concentration, long-term UV irradiation and various pH values. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity changed only slightly after 5 h of UV light irradiation, indicating that N-CQDs had good photostability. In Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs did not change significantly even in NaCl solution of up to 1 mol/L, indicating that N-CQDs has good tolerance to high ionic strength. The fluorescence intensity did not decrease drastically in the pH range of 1–14 (HCl and NaOH were used as factors to control pH), and there was a maximum emission intensity at pH = 6 (Fig. 4f). In summary, the prepared N-CQDs possessed excellent stability, which demonstrated that the proposed N-CQDs had great potential for application in natural environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of TC, OTC and CTC
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments for tetracycline antibiotic determination were performed at pH = 7 due to the initial pH value of the prepared N-CQDs solution of 7, considering the simplification of the operation steps. The incubation time has a great influence on the assay results, so the assay incubation time was investigated, as shown in Fig. S2. After adding the target, the fluorescence of the system reached the extreme value in a very short time (0.5 min), and there was no significant change within 5 min. To obtain accurate assay results, 5 min was chosen as the incubation time. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually decreased as the TC concentration increased from 0 to 100 μM, with a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and TC in the concentration range of 0–30 μM and 30–100 μM. The fitted linear equations were F/F0 = 0.960–0.020 C[TC] (R2 = 0.990) and F/F0 = −0.080 + 1.037/[1 + (C[TC]/26)1.468] (R2 =0.995), respectively, and the LOD was 0.344 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, the response of OTC to the fluorescence of system was similar to that of TC. The fluorescence intensity possessed a good linear relationship with OTC in the concentration range of 0–20 μM and 20–100 μM. The fitted linear equations were F/F0 = 1.012–0.026 C[TC] (R2 = 0.983) and F/F0 = −0.073 + 1.093/[1 + (C[OTC]/22.193)1.289] (R2 = 0.998), respectively, and the LOD was 0.265 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5d). Different from TC and OTC, CTC caused a significant increase in systemic fluorescence at low concentrations (0–40 μM) ( Fig. 6a), however, it gradually decreases the system fluorescence at higher concentrations (40–400 μM) (Fig. 6b). The fluorescence intensity possessed a good linear relationship with CTC in the concentration range of 0–40 μM and 40–200 μM. The fitted linear equations were F/F0 = 1.113 + 0.059 C[CTC] (R2 = 0.985) and F/F0 = 4.187–0.017 C[CTC] (R2 = 0.990), respectively, and the LOD was 0.117 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 6c).

The selectivity and anti-interference investigations are extremely important elements of the sensor feasibility study. Therefore, common metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Hg2+) and biomolecules (L-Cys, Gly, Glucose, L-Phe, L-Ser, L-Ala, L-Asp, L-Lys, AA, L-Arg, FA, UA, Urea, DA, GSH, H2O2, AMOX, CRO, PG) were used to investigate the selectivity and interference resistance of this sensing system. The result was shown in Fig. 6d, which indicated that the system had good selectivity and anti-interference properties for tetracycline antibiotics. Moreover, the proposed method can detect more tetracycline antibiotics with comparable sensitivity and selectivity compared to the reported method ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for the N-CQDs response to TC, OTC and CTC
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the sensing mechanism of N-CQDs for tetracycline antibiotics, the UV absorption of tetracycline antibiotics was measured. As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorption spectra of the three tetracycline antibiotics showed a large overlap with the excitation spectra of N-CQDs, which was consistent with the characteristics of the IFE [32]. The 3D fluorescence spectra of the system containing antibiotics were further measured, it can be seen that the fluorescence located at 360 nm excitation in the 3D fluorescence image was reduced, as shown in Fig. 7b–d and f. For CTC, the fluorescence of the system was significantly enhanced when the concentration was 0–40 μM (Fig. 7e), which may be due to the fact that a small amount of CTC can passivate the surface defects of N-CQDs and make the fluorescence enhanced, while when the concentration exceeded 40 μM, the IFE of CTC was revealed and the fluorescence was reduced. The specific reasons for this will be further investigated in future work. In addition, it can be seen from the 3D fluorescence image that only the fluorescence with excitation wavelength near 360 nm was quenched, so it was most reasonable to perform the detection experiment under the excitation of 360 nm. The characteristics of IFE-induced fluorescence quenching is that the fluorescence lifetime does not change significantly and no new material is produced [19]. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime and UV absorption of N-CQDs and N-CQDs+TC were measured. As shown in Fig. 8, the results of the measures were shown in Table S2, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the two almost overlapped, and the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs+TC were 6.45 ns and 6.06 ns, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, after adding the detection object, there was no new absorption peak in the UV absorption spectrum of the mixed system, which proved that no new substance was generated. All the above results proved that the sensing mechanism was IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC, OTC and CTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed probe, TC/OTC/CTC levels of rat serum samples and milk samples were monitored. The various concentrations of TC/OTC/CTC were spiked in the tested rat serum samples based on the standard addition method, and the detection results were summarized in Table 2. The recoveries of TC/OTC/CTC ranged from 94.86 % to 109.86 %. The satisfactory recoveries confirmed that the developed fluorescent probe was suitable for the detection of TC/OTC/CTC in real samples and had potential practical applications.

In summary, an excellent N-CQDs fluorescence probe for detection tetracycline antibiotics were synthesized successfully by one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose and ethylenediamine as precursors. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence and excellent stability. The N-CQDs were further used to detect TC/OTC/CTC with a concentration range of 0–100/0–100/0–200 μM with the LOD of 0.344/0.265/ 0.117 μM, and showed good selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability. In addition, N-CQDs have been successfully applied to actual rat serum and milk samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 94.86 % to 109.86 %. The results showed that the fluorescent probe proposed in this work can be used to detect tetracycline antibiotics in real samples and has excellent potential for practical applications.
Cunjin Wang: Writing – original draft. Qian Sun: Conceptualization. Min Yang: Investigation. Enzhou Liu: Investigation. Weiming Xue: Funding acquisition. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276). The authors would like to thank not only the Key Science and Technology Innovation Team of Shaanxi Province (2022TD-3) for their help in the experiments, but also the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129982.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of tetracycline residues in shrimp and whole milk using liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection and residue confirmation by mass spectrometry
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.C. Andersen, J.E. Roybal, S.A. Gonzales, S.B. Turnipseed, A.P. Pfenning, L.R. Kuck, Determination of tetracycline residues in shrimp and whole milk using liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection and residue confirmation by mass spectrometry, Analytica Chimica Acta 529(1) (2005) 145–150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline antibiotics and pH discrimination from aggregation-induced emission mechanism
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, L. Shi, J. Jia, O. Eltayeb, S. Shuang, Red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline antibiotics and pH discrimination from aggregation-induced emission mechanism, Sensors and Actuators B Chemical 332 (2021) 129513.

Environ. Sci. Technol.

Zhao, Comprehensive Evaluation of Antibiotics Emission and Fate in the River Basins of China: Source Analysis, Multimedia Modeling, and Linkage to Bacterial Resistance, Environmental Science & Technology 49(11) (2015) 6772–6782.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric determination of doxycycline based on the use of carbon quantum dots incorporated into a molecularly imprinted polymer
